coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of substituted Y-type hexaferrites/carbon dots composites for recording media and photodegradation of dye
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Kamran
Batool, Zahida
Manzoor, Sumaira
Ahmad, Dilshad
Aman, Salma
Alhashmialameer, Dalal
Abd El-Gawad, Hala H.
Taha, T.A.
Ashiq, Muhammad Naeem
coredata.dc:description: The normal microemulsion approach was employed for the synthesis of SrBaZn2-xMnxFe12-2ySnyNiyO22 (x = 0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1.0) while the hydrothermal method was used to synthesize the carbon dots (CDs) and their nanocomposite. The structural characteristics, architectures and surface area of the materials are examined by using various techniques. The magnetic features i.e. magnetic remanence (Mr) and saturation magnetization (Ms) increase up to x,y = 0.5 while the coercivity (Hc) decreases which is explained based on-site occupation of substituents and super-exchange interactions. The electrical resistivity of the substituted ferrite materials also increased from 24.62 to 46.76 × 106 Ω cm. The increment in saturation magnetization, remanence, and electrical resistivity suggests that ferrite material can be utilized for high-density recording media as well as for microwave devices. The composite materials with composition SrBaZn1 .5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22/CDs with different ratios have been employed as photocatalysts for the degradation of Rhodamine B under sunlight. Rhodamine B (RhB) was effectively degraded by photocatalysts with a low CDs content i.e. 5.0 wt percent. The composite degrades the dye up to 96% in 80 min and also showed excellent stability after five consecutive runs. The composite material can be employed for the removal of environmental pollutants using natural sunlight for future applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Rhodamine B
Magnetic properties
Electrical resistivity
Composite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns for SrBaZn1-xMnxFe12-2ySnyNiyO22 and the composite material.
SEM images for (a) = SrBaZn2Fe12O22, (b) = SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22, (c) = carbon dots and (d) = CDs/SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 composite.
BET isotherms for SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22, carbon dots and CDs/SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 composite.
Hysteresis loops for all the synthesized materials.
Variation of dc electrical resistivity with composition.
UV/visible spectra and Tauc plots for (a, b) = CDS, (c, d) = ferrite and (e, f) = composite.
Photoluminescence (PL) spectra for carbon dots, ferrite and composite material.
(a) = % photocatalytic activity of CDs, Ferrite and composite with different ratio and effect of time on the degradation of RhB (b) = CDs, (c) = Ferrite and (d) = composite.
(a) = percentage degradation of RhB in the presence of scavengers, (b) = reusability and stability test and (c) = XRD pattern of the composite before and after five consecutive runs.
Schematic illustration for the photodegradation mechanism of RhB.
Cell parameters (a &c, Vcell), crystallite size (S) and dc electrical resistivity for SrBaZn1-xMnxFe12-2ySnyNiyO22.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of substituted Y-type hexaferrites/carbon dots composites for recording media and photodegradation of dye

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The normal microemulsion approach was employed for the synthesis of SrBaZn2-xMnxFe12-2ySnyNiyO22 (x = 0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1.0) while the hydrothermal method was used to synthesize the carbon dots (CDs) and their nanocomposite. The structural characteristics, architectures and surface area of the materials are examined by using various techniques. The magnetic features i.e. magnetic remanence (Mr) and saturation magnetization (Ms) increase up to x,y = 0.5 while the coercivity (Hc) decreases which is explained based on-site occupation of substituents and super-exchange interactions. The electrical resistivity of the substituted ferrite materials also increased from 24.62 to 46.76 × 106 Ω cm. The increment in saturation magnetization, remanence, and electrical resistivity suggests that ferrite material can be utilized for high-density recording media as well as for microwave devices. The composite materials with composition SrBaZn1 .5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22/CDs with different ratios have been employed as photocatalysts for the degradation of Rhodamine B under sunlight. Rhodamine B (RhB) was effectively degraded by photocatalysts with a low CDs content i.e. 5.0 wt percent. The composite degrades the dye up to 96% in 80 min and also showed excellent stability after five consecutive runs. The composite material can be employed for the removal of environmental pollutants using natural sunlight for future applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials (1–100 nm size) are now recognized as an emerging and fascinating class of materials due to their amazing properties. These have outstanding mechanical, electrical, thermal, magnetic, and optical capabilities due to their small size, enhanced surface energy, and specific surface area [1]. It is because of their structural features that nanoparticles have been able to widen their range of usual uses. These materials can be used in energy storage devices, electronics, sensors, magnetic materials, and photocatalysts owing to their unique physicochemical properties. Electrochemical, electrical, and dielectric characteristics, as well as catalytic and magnetic capabilities, are all hallmarks of ferrite materials [2,3]. They might be useful for various applications from electronic gadgets to photocatalysts to microwaves to high-frequency equipment. According to magnetic chemistry, ferrites are divided into hard and soft subclasses [4]. On the basis of their crystal structure, they are categorized into garnet, spinel, orthogonal, and hexagonal ferrites. Based on their hexagonal structure and varied composition, they are categorized into six types: M-type (BaFe12O19), W-type (BaMe2Fe16O27), X-type (Ba2Me2Fe28O46), Y-type (Ba2Me2Fe12O22), Z-type (Ba3Me2Fe24O41), and U-type (Ba4Me2Fe36O60) [5] where Me is the divalent cations.

Electronic devices now operate at gigahertz frequencies due to the inherent constraints imposed by the occurrence of their naturally occurring ferromagnetic resonance at ultra-high frequencies, therefore, the conventional soft magnetic ferrites i.e., spinel ferrite cannot be used at this high frequency because of their low dc electrical resistivity [6]. As a result of this advancement in technology, Y-type hexaferrites have the potential to replace spinel ferrites in applications over 1 GHz [7]. In recent years, efforts have been done to alter the structural, electrical, and magnetic properties of Y and M-type hexaferrites by replacing divalent or trivalent ions and their binary combinations. As a result, the substitution of various ions at iron as well as at divalent cation sites have been studied, including Al-Ga [8], In Ref. [9], Zn [10], Al [11], Cu [12], Mg [13], Pb [14], Al-In Ref. [15], Mn [16], In Ref. [17] etc, and also the combination of tetravalent and divalent ions such as Ni2+–Zr4+ [18]. In most of cases, if magnetic properties were improved then the electrical properties have been reduced at the same time [19]. So there are very few examples in which both the properties were enhanced. In the present work, the main aim was to enhance the electrical properties so that the substituted material can be used at high frequency. The second major aim was to improve the magnetic properties such as saturation magnetization and remanence as well as to reduce the coercivity which is beneficial for their use in high density recording media.

Recently, water pollution has become a major threat worldwide due to the rapid increase in number of industries and most of the industries such as paper, textile, food, printing, pharmaceutical, and cosmetics use dyes for improving the quality of the products [20–22]. These all industries produce a lot of color wastewater which is dropped in the lakes, rivers, etc. near to that industry. These colors are non-biodegradable, carcinogenic in nature as well as genotoxic for human and aquatic life. Rhodamine B dye is radish violet in color and is widely used in the textile and food industries [23]. This dye cause irritation to the eyes, skin and respiratory tract and also the carcinogenicity, neurotoxicity and reproductive toxicity of this dye has been proven experimentally [24]. Therefore, it is necessary to remove this dye from the wastewater to save human and aquatic life.

Various techniques have been used for the removal of coloring substances from the wastewater such as adsorption [25], coagulation [26], advanced oxidation [27] and photocatalytic degradation [28–30]. Among all, photocatalytic degradation is one of the clean and eco-friendly to remove the dyes from the wastewater. For this purpose, various oxides semiconductor photocatalysts have used such as ZnO [31], NiO [32], CuO [33],Fe2O3 [34], Bi2WO6 [35] and rGO-Fe3O4/TiO2 [36] etc. The magnetic oxide materials such as spinel ferrite with the general formula MFe2O4 (where M = divalent cation such as Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, etc) are attracting much attention now-a-days to be used as photocatalysts due to their excellent chemical and photo-corrosion stability [37]. Many researchers modified these materials to improve the photocatalytic efficiency by doping, substituting and forming a composite with other materials so that the charge separation may be enhanced. Among the magnetic materials, the hexaferrites specially Y-type hexaferrites have superior characteristics to those of the spinel ferrites such as higher magnetic moment, higher stability toward chemicals and heat, higher electrical resistivity to be used in microwave devices and excellent corrosion stability [38]. The Y-type hexaferrites are very much less explored for the photocatalytic process, therefore, in the present work, the substituted Y-type hexaferrite has been chosen along with the carbon dots. Among all other carbon allotropes, the carbon dots (CDs) have excellent chemical and photo-physical properties including, a wide range of wavelength light absorption, chemical inertness, low toxicity, photosensitization, easy functionalization, photosensitization and a verity of facile synthetic methods [39]. Due to all these characteristics, the carbon dots have been chosen for the formation of composite with ferrite material. Carbon dots having lower band gap value not only absorb more light but also inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electrons/holes pairs which will enhance the efficiency of the composite material.

In the current investigation, we fabricated Y-type hexaferrites with composition SrBaZn1-xMnxFe12-2ySnyNiyO22 (x = 0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75 and 1) and their structural, electrical and magnetic characteristics were investigated. The material with composition SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 showed superior electrical and magnetic properties as compared to all other materials and therefore this composition has been selected for the fabrication of composite with carbon dots (CDs). The composite with a ratio 1:0.5 (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22:CDs) showed higher photocatalytic activity as compared to other composites and the photocatalytic mechanism has also been studied.

Simple micro-emulsion approach was used to synthesize Y type hexaferrite SrBaZn2-xMnxFe12-2ySnyNiyO22 with variation in x,y as 0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75 and 1.0. Precursor metal nitrates were dissolved in deionized water to get the required concentrations of solution i.e. iron nitrate nonahydrate (0.1 M, 4.04 g), Sr and barium nitrate (0.0083 M, 0.217 g) and zinc nitrate hexahydrate (0.017 M, 0.506 g) when x,y = 0.0 and the concentrations of the substituents were changed accordingly. All these solutions were stirred on a magnetic hot plate and then CTAB was added to the solution as a surfactant with ratio metal:CTAB (1:1.5). All of the sample mixtures were held at a pH of 11–12 by the addition of 2 M KOH (11.22 g) solution dropwise to get the precipitates. The mixture was further stirred for two more hours to homogenize it. After removing the precipitates by centrifugation process and then washing with deionized water several times until the pH is down to 7. The precipitates were dried at 373 K in an oven and then annealed for 9 h at 1223 K using Vulcan A-550 muffle furnace. The powdered samples were stored and applied for further characterization.

Carbon Dots (CDs) was fabricated using a hydrothermal route in which 40 mL of milk was mixed with 32 mL of water. This mixture was then stirred for 20 min on a magnetic hotplate vigorously. The mixture of milk and water was then poured into the autoclave of 100 mL capacity and filled up to its 80% capacity. The autoclave e was placed at 453 K in an electric oven for 2 h and then the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The obtained precipitates were centrifuged, rinsed and washed several times with deionized water and last with ethanol. The product was then dried at 323 K and stored for further characterization.

The composite of the Y-type hexaferrite having composition SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 with carbon dots have been synthesized with different ratio 1:0, 1:0.25, 1:0.5 and 1:0.75 of SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22:CDs have been synthesized by the hydrothermal method. The milk (50 mL) was diluted with 40 mL of water under constant stirring. After that, the weight amount of already synthesized hexaferrite (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22) was added to the milk/water mixture and then stirred for about 2 h. Then the whole mixture was transferred to an autoclave and heat it at 453 K for 2 h. The autoclave was cool down to room temperature, the precipitates were centrifuged and washed several times with DI water. Finally, they were dried at 323 K in an oven and stored for further characterization.

A Rigaku D/max-Ultima X-ray diffractometer working at 40 KV and 40 mA, from 2θ = 20 to 80°) was used for phase confirmation of all the synthesized materials. By utilizing a scanning electron microscope (JSM-5600LV) the morphology of the materials was confirmed while the surface area was confirmed by BET analysis using Micrometrics 2460. The optical properties of materials were studied by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV759S) and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy using confocal micro-Pl/Raman spectrophotometer. The magnetic properties were investigated by using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM lakeshore-7900) while the dc electrical resistivity was measured by a two-point probe method using Kiethly-2400 source meter. The magnetic field was applied parallel to the easy c-axis. The pellets of 13 mm and 2.5 mm in thickness were used for the measurement of electrical resistivity.

The photocatalytic activity of the nanomaterials was studied by degrading RhB in an aqueous solution. Sunlight was recreated by using a 350 W Xe lamp with a complete spectrum distribution and an ultraviolet (UV) cut-off filter at 290 nm. A 50.00 mL RhB solution and 10.00 mg L−1 of the prepared photocatalyst were also added. Using a magnet, the solution was agitated in dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption and desorption equilibrium among RhB and the nanocomposites. The light was on and after a 20-min time interval, 3.00 mL samples were obtained, and the catalyst was removed through centrifugation. The concentration of RhB in the samples was determined using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV759S). Some blank experiments were also performed to confirm that the degradation of dye is due to the presence of photocatalyst and light. For this purpose, the dye solution was kept under sunlight without the addition of a photocatalyst while in the second part the photocatalyst was added to the dye solution in dark. In both cases, it was observed that there is little change in the absorbance which confirmed that the degradation of dye is due to the process of photocatalysis.

The powder X-ray diffraction analysis was carried out to confirm the hexagonal phase formation of the fabricated materials. Fig. 1 represents the XRD patterns of all the substituted materials as well as the composite material (CDs/SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22). All the diffracted peaks are well matched with the standard pattern JCPDS No. 00-025-1193, having no extra peak indicating the single hexagonal formation of the prepared materials. The peak in the composite material at around 2θ = 26° is related to the carbon dots with hkl value (002) as reported earlier which confirms that the composite has been successfully formed.

Various parameters have been investigated from the XRD data such as cell volume (Vcell), lattice constants (a, c), and size of crystallite (S) has been calculated from the well-known equations as reported earlier [40]. The lattice constants (a & c) and the cell volume increase with the substituents content as tabulated in Table 1 . The enhancement of cell parameters with substituents concentration can be explained on the basis of their ionic radii. The ionic radii of the substituted metal ions such as Mn2+ (80 p.m.), Ni2+ (72 p.m.) and Sn4+ (75 p.m.) are higher as compared to host metal ions i.e. Zn2+ (74 p.m.) and Fe3+ (64 p.m.). The higher ionic radii of the dopants are responsible for the increase in the cell parameters. The crystallite size of the prepared materials calculated from the Scherer equation is ranging from 30 to 50 nm which can also be indicated from the peak broadening in the XRD pattern. The composite material has smaller crystallite size (37 nm) which is responsible for the high surface area which is beneficial for catalytic applications.

The surface morphology and the particle size of the fabricated materials were examined by the scanning electron microscopic (SEM) techniques and their images are shown in Fig. 2 a–d. The particles are in cubic shape as given in Fig. 2a&b for SrBaZnFe12O22 and SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22, respectively. The cubes shaped particles are distributed homogeneously for materials with x, y = 0.5 (Fig. 2b) and the grain size calculated through Image j is found to be in the 40–50 nm range. The small nanoparticles have been agglomerated in the case of carbon dots (Fig. 2c) while in the composite small particles of ferrite materials are decorated on the surface of the carbon dots (Fig. 2d) having porous morphology. The development of heterojunction between SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 and CDs photocatalyst material is depicted in Fig. 2d. It is very difficult to distinguish the heterojunction formation between the CD and the SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 nanocomposite from SEM analysis due to the porous nature of the CDs, and is evidently exposed that the ferrite cubes bonded with the porous nature particles, responsible for the remarkable activity due to numerous numbers of active sites. The surface area of ferrite (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22), carbon dots (CDs) and their composite were investigated by BET analysis and their N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms are given in Fig. 3 . The composite material (330 m2 g−1) has a much higher surface area than individual material i.e. SrBaZn1.5Mn0.5Fe11Sn0.5Ni0.5O22 (207 m2 g−1) and carbon dots (225 m2 g−1). The higher surface area of the composite indicates that there will be a large number of active sites on the surface of the composite which is the primary requirement to be used as a photocatalyst.

The magnetic properties of all the fabricated materials were investigated by the measurement of hysteresis loops and magnetic parameters such as remanence (Mr), saturation magnetization (MS) and coercivity (Hc) were measured. All the material hysteresis loops are given in Fig. 4 and the narrow loop of the unsubstituted ferrite indicates it is in the soft ferrite category [41]. The calculated values of Ms, Mr and Hc are tabulated in Table 1 and one can see that the Ms and Mr increase with the increase in substituents level up to x, y = 0.5 and then started to decrease. This variation of these magnetic parameters can be explicated on the origin of the distribution of cations on various sub-lattice sites. The structure of the Y-type hexaferrites contain 3 TS blocks with the general formula S = Me2 2+Fe4 3+ O8 and T = Ba2 2+Fe8 3+O14 and in a unit cell, there are three formula units. Here all the ferric ions (36 Fe3+) in Y-type hexaferrite unit cell are distributed at the different sub-lattice site and their coordination and number of ions are given in bracket i.e. 3avI (octahedral, 1), 18hVI (Octahedral, 6), 6cvI (octahedral, 2), 3bv1 (octahedral, 1) 6c1v (tetrahedral, 2), 6cIV (tetrahedral, 2) [42]. The three 3av1, 18hVI and 3bv1 have spin in an upward direction and the other three have spin downward. It is reported that the Sn4+ covers the tetrahedral site while the Ni2+ goes to the octahedral site [43]. The Sn4+ has zero unpaired electrons while Ni2+ has three unpaired electrons according to their electronic configuration. When tin (IV) replaced the ferric ions (having 5 unpaired electrons) from the tetrahedral site then the total number of unpaired electrons in the upward direction increased as all the tetrahedral sites spin in the downward direction. Although the Ni(II) replace the ferric from the octahedral site as a whole after compensation of opposite spins the total number of unpaired e− increase in the upward direction which are responsible for the increment in both the saturation magnetization as well as the remanence. Secondly, the Zn2+ (with zero unpaired electrons) is also replaced by the Mn2+ (having 5 unpaired electrons) is also responsible for the increase in both the parameters. The Ms and Mr for the composite material (CDs/SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22) with a ratio 1:0.5 showed lower values of both the parameters than that of the ferrite (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22). These lower values may be due to the presence of the non-magnetic carbon dots. The reduction in both parameters after x,y = 0.5 is owing to the reduction in super-exchange interactions due to the higher content of non-magnetic Sn(IV) ions.

The squareness ratio (Mr/Ms) was also calculated and their values are given in Table 1. This ratio has varied from 0.42 to 0.71 which is less than the ideal value of 1 where all particles are supposed to be in a single magnetic phase. It also has been reported in the literature that if the value of the squareness ratio is greater than 0.5 then most of the particles will be in a single domain [44] and here in the present investigation most of the materials have a ratio greater than 0.5, suggesting the single magnetic domain distribution of the particles. As the magnetic field was applied parallel to the easy c-axis, the electrons aligned in the same direction and the magnetization is in the uniform direction. The coercivity (Hc) of the fabricated materials was also calculated and is clear from Table 1 that its value decreases with the substitution level. There is an inverse relationship between the Ms and Hc i.e. Hc = K1/μoMs, in the present work, the coercivity results are almost satisfactory with this relation i.e. higher saturation magnetization lower is the value of Hc and vice versa. The increment in Ms and Mr while the reduction in Hc values indicates that the materials with composition SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 are best among all other materials for its application in high density recording media. Therefore, this composition (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22) has been chosen for the preparation of composite with carbon dots.

The DC electrical resistivity of the fabricated materials was evaluated by the two point probe technique and its variation with substituents contents is shown in Fig. 5 . Although the four point probe method is more sophisticated as compared to two point probe method for highly resistive materials such as ferrites, the two point probe method has been used widely. As the sample in the four point probe method is connected with the silver paste and for the highly resistive materials which need high voltage, the sample may be heated which melts the silver paste and lost the connection. Therefore, the four point probe method is used for the materials which are highly conductive and need lower voltage as well as it is used at low temperatures. The resistivity of the ferrites increases from 24.62 to 46.76 × 106 Ω cm with the dopants concentration and reached a maximum value at x,y = 0.5 and then starts to decrease. In Y-type hexaferrites the conduction mechanism is the exchange of electrons from ferric (Fe3+) to ferrous ions (Fe2+) at the octahedral site [61]. When nickel ions replaced the ferric ions from the octahedral site then the total number of iron ions at that site decreased as well as the hopping of electrons and subsequently, the conductivity decreased. The conduction mechanism can be depicted as:
Fe3+ + Ni2+ ↔ Fe2+ + Ni3+

Now the conduction mechanism is electron hopping between ferric to ferrous ions as well the hopping of holes due to oxidation of nickel ions but the hopping of holes is more difficult as related to electrons and consequently the resistivity of the material increase. At a higher substitution level the tin (IV) ions that were replacing the iron ions from the tetrahedral site now start to push a few iron ions from the tetrahedral to the octahedral site and as a result the concentration of ferric ions increases which is responsible for the increase in conductivity but at a lower concentration, this effect may be very small and did not affect the resistivity. The resistivity of the composite material is lower as compared to SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 which may be due to the higher conductivity of the carbon dots as compared to ferrite material. In carbon dots the C–C sp2 hybridization that will permit the overlapping of π electrons of adjacent carbon atoms and ferrite surface sites which enhance the conductivity of the composite materials. The increase in resistivity of the ferrite material (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22) suggests that this material can be used for microwave devices application as these devices required highly resistive materials [45].

Fig. 6 depicts the photocatalyst's optical properties and diffuse reflectance spectra for all the fabricated materials i.e. substituted hexaferrite (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22), carbon dots (CDs) and their composite (CDs/SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22) with ratio 0.5/1.0 and their band gaps were determined from the Tauc plots. All the UV/visible spectra edges showed the absorption in the visible spectrum and the band gap for the ferrite material calculated from the Tauc plot is found to be 3.0 eV, for CDs 2.36 eV while for composite material its value is 1.86 eV. The band gap value for the composite material is lower as compared to individual materials i.e. CDs and SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 which indicates that the composite material can absorb more light in the visible part of the solar spectrum. This lower band gap value suggests that we can use sunlight as a source of light because it contains 45% of the visible region which is the basic requirement to choose a photocatalyst to work under sunlight [46,47].

Photogenerated charge carrier entrapment, migration, and separation can be investigated using the photoluminescence (PL) analysis. Fig. 7 shows that for the hexaferrite (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22) material the fluorescence intensity was very high indicating that there are high electrons/holes recombination as the intensity of the PL spectrum is directly proportional to the charge carriers recombination [48]. The peak intensity in the PL spectrum of the composite is lower than all other materials indicating that it has a higher capacity for electrons/holes separation which can enhance the photocatalytic property of the composite material.

To estimate the photocatalytic activity of synthesized materials under direct sunlight irradiation, Rhodamine B (RhB) was used as a target pollutant which is commonly used in various industries. Some blanks experiments were conducted to confirm that the photocatalyst and the sunlight are the major contributors to the degradation of the studied dye. Firstly, the dye solution of 20 ppm was irradiated under sunlight without the addition of a catalyst. Secondly, 0.01 g of catalyst was added to 100 mL of dye solution in the dark and stirred for half an hour. In both the experiments no degradation occurred but observed a minute change in the absorbance of the dye solution which may be due to adsorption on the surface of the photocatalyst. These both blank experiments confirmed that the degradation of dye is due to the presence of photocatalyst as well as sunlight. The percentage degradation of the dye has been calculated by the following equation [49]:
% degradation = (1 - Ct/Co) × 100
Where Ct is the concentration at time t and Co is the initial concentration of the dye at t = 0. The percentage degradation of the dyes using various photocatalysts i.e. carbon dots, substituted hexaferrite (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22) and their composite with the different ratios is shown in Fig. 8 a. The carbon dots and substituted hexaferrite (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22) showed the photodegradation efficiency of 43% and 63%, respectively. The efficiency of the composite materials with 1:0.25, 1:0.5 and 1:0.75 ratio of SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22:CDs was also investigated and found to be 77, 96 and 85%, respectively. The variation of absorbance as a function of time for all the fabricated materials is also shown in Fig. 8b–d. The absorbance decrease with the time and this reduction is maximum in the case of optimized composite material (SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22/CDs) with a 1/0.5 ratio indicating that the composite material showed better efficiency (96%) as compared to individual materials. The photocatalytic efficiency decrease when the concentration of carbon dots increases from a 1:0.5 ratio which may be due to the shielding effect which restricts the ferrite to absorb more light [50,51]. The higher efficiency of the composite material is also due to the smaller size of the composite material which has higher active sites on its surface for the adsorption of dye which is the initial step for the photodegradation.

The photocatalysts under sunlight produce e−/h+ pair by absorption of light and shifting of electrons from valence to conduction band exiting holes in the valence band. These photogenerated charge species then react with oxygen and water to produce reactive species which degrade the dye [52]. The lower efficiency of the carbon dots and hexaferrite is because of the recombination of electrons and holes pair and the production of less number of reactive species (•O− 2 and •OH). Therefore, the higher efficiency of the ferrite materials as compared to carbon dots may be due to the photo-Fenton reaction. The electrons in the conduction band of the hexaferrite after absorption of light react with the ferric ions and converted into ferrous ions [53]. In the presence of a ferric/ferrous ion pair, the Fenton reaction produces more •OH and as a result, the SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 showed intermediate photodegradation efficiency of 63%.

The composite material with the ratio 1/0.5 of hexaferrite/carbon dots showed maximum efficiency which can be explained on the basis of PL analysis. The peak intensity in the PL spectrum is minimum in the case of the composite material which confirmed that the recombination of charge carriers is minimum and is responsible for the higher activity. When the CDs were attached with ferrites based materials, then the red-shifted emission could find at λ = 401–414 nm as depicted in Fig. 5b. Consequently, CDs/ferrite can proceed photocatalysis under visible irradiations. Composites containing CDs have better photocatalytic properties than other common photocatalysts like oxides and chalcogenides. On the other hand, CDs particles are less toxic, chemically stable, and water soluble. CDs in the near-infrared (NIR) range can act as an effective catalyst because high-energy radiation can damage organic molecules. The better efficiency of the ferrite/CDs as compared to other photocatalysts can be illustrated using these concepts. (a) Under visible/sunlight light irradiation, π-conjugated CDs potentially operate as a sensitizer by donating their photo-excited electrons to the conduction band of ferrite materials (b) Through consuming photo-excited electrons from ferrite, CDs can function as an electron reservoir, suppressing e/h + recombination; (c) CDs could also aid in the binding of organic pollutants via the π-π* interaction, increasing the chances of degradation. Furthermore, CDs could perform photocatalytic activity responses due to the substantial-conjugation located inside the aromatic rings of dye, which reduces the energy gap between the valence and conduction bands. To further investigate the photocatalytic mechanism, the holes, •O− 2 and •OH scavenger experiments were performed as shown in Fig. 9 a. The photocatalytic activity was reduced from 96% to 41%, 27% and 60% when used EDTA-2Na as a hole scavenger, tertiary butanol as •OH scavenger and para-benzoquinone as •O− 2 scavenger, respectively. The maximum reduction in photocatalytic activity (96%–27%) was observed in the case of •OH scavenger experiment which confirmed that the major reactive species is •OH which degrade the RhB while the holes and •O− 2 are the minor contributors.

The possible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B is given in Fig. 10 . In the first step, the photocatalyst absorbs the sunlight and electrons/holes pair is generated. The electron then reacts with O2 to produce the •O− 2 and the reactive specie •O− 2 then reacts with a proton which produces the •OH radicles. At the same time, the photo-Fenton reaction also produced a large amount of •OH reactive species and holes are left behind. In this way, an appreciable quantity of the •OH is produced which is responsible for the higher efficiency of the composite material. The following equations can explain the possible mechanism:

SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22/CDs + hυ
→
SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 (e−/h+) + CDs (e−/h+).
Fe+3 + e− → Fe+2 (photo-reduction reaction).
CDs (e−) + O2 → •O− 2 - (Photocatalytic reaction)

The stability of the photocatalyst and its reusability is as important as their efficiency. The stability, as well as the reusability of the photocatalyst, was also studied in the present work. Centrifugation was used to extract the SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22/CDs photocatalyst from the dye solution after irradiating it under sunlight for 80 min and was subsequently cleaned three times with deionized water and dried. The dried photocatalyst was reused five times and Fig. 9b depicts the SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22/CDs nanocomposite recycling periods throughout the RhB photodegradation process. It was observed that there is little decrease in the catalytic efficiency after consecutive five runs (Fig. 9b). The XRD analysis was also carried out for the photocatalyst after consecutive five runs and is given in Fig. 9c. All the peaks in the XRD pattern retained at the same 2θ position as was before photocatalytic runs which confirmed that the crystal structure of the photocatalyst remain the same and showed excellent stability.

The substituted Y-type hexaferrite nanomaterials were successfully fabricated by the normal microemulsion method. XRD analysis was used to confirm the hexagonal phase while the morphology, surface area and band gap were confirmed by the SEM, BET and UV/visible spectroscopy. The Ms, Mr and Hc were obtained from the hysteresis loops and dc electrical resistivity was measured by the two-point probe method. The saturation magnetization and remanence increase from 16.7 to 48 emu g−1 and 8.5–31 emu g−1, respectively while the coercivity decrease from 2.05 to 0.6 KOe. The electrical resistivity increased from 24.62 to 46.76 × 106 Ω cm with the substituents concentration. The improvement in magnetic and electric parameters suggests that the materials with composition SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 can be used in recording media and microwave devices. The composite of the SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22 with carbon dots has also been fabricated with various ratios and excellent results for the photodegradation of RhB up to 96% were obtained for the sample with SrBaZn1·5Mn0·5Fe11Sn0·5Ni0·5O22:CDS (1:0–5). Some trapping experiments were also performed to confirm the photodegradation mechanism and the composite material showed excellent stability as well. This composite material can be used for further commercial applications to degrade the Rhodamine B dye from the aqueous media.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The manuscript has not been published previously, and is not under consideration for publication elsewhere. It is also stated that the work is original. The publication is approved by all authors and tacitly or explicitly by the responsible authorities where the work was carried out. If accepted, it will not be published elsewhere in the same form, in English or in any other language, including electronically without the written consent of the copyright-holder. In addition, the authors have no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research at King Khalid University for funding this work through General Research Project under grant number (R.G.P. 2/114/43). Dr Zahida Batool and kamran Khan are grateful to higher education commission of Pakistan for NRPU 9460.
